output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Critique with targeted revisions. The section overclaims novelty, conflates two distinct contributions, and leaves several evidence gaps that a reviewer will flag immediately.

**Overclaiming and unsupported novelty**

The phrase "first principled treatment of synthesis-time uncertainty in program induction" is almost certainly false and is the single most damaging line in the section. Calibration of code/program models has been studied — e.g., work on confidence estimation for neural program synthesis, verifier-reranked sampling (AlphaCode-style), execution-based filtering, and broader literatures on selective prediction (Geifman & El-Yaniv) and temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017). Temperature scaling itself is a 2017 technique; applying it to a code head is an engineering contribution, not a foundational one. Similarly, "fills a gap that prior LLM-based code-generation work has left open" and "we expect it to become a standard component" are forward-looking marketing, not claims a reviewer will accept.

Suggested rewrite of the contribution framing:

> We present CalibSynth, which augments a transformer code model with a temperature-scaled confidence head trained to produce per-program calibration scores. Compared to using raw softmax likelihood or beam-frequency as a confidence proxy, CalibSynth substantially reduces expected calibration error and improves selective accuracy on HumanEval-style benchmarks. Our contribution is empirical: a simple, low-overhead calibration layer that makes program-level deferral decisions reliable enough to be useful in IDE-integrated tooling.

**Conflated contributions**

The section bundles three different claims: (a) a calibration method, (b) a deferral/human-in-the-loop use case, and (c) a prediction about adoption. Only (a) is evaluated. Either evaluate the deferral utility directly (e.g., end-to-end task success when low-confidence outputs are routed to a human, or cost-savings under a fixed reviewer budget) or drop the human-deferral framing from the contribution statement and present it as motivation only.

**Evidence-claim gaps**

- ECE 0.043 vs. 0.18/0.22: no confidence intervals, no number of bins, no calibration-evaluation protocol, no dataset size. ECE is famously sensitive to binning; without this, the headline number is not reproducible.
- "Selective accuracy at 50% coverage is 91.2% vs 78.3%": which baseline is the "strongest"? Report both. And report the full risk–coverage curve or AURC, not a single operating point.
- "Held-out subset of HumanEval-style problems" is vague. Is it HumanEval, MBPP, a custom split, or a paraphrased variant? Report exact benchmark, split sizes, and whether the temperature scalar was re-fit on this distribution (this matters because Section 5 admits per-task-family refitting).
- The Codex-style baseline is undefined (which model, which decoding setup, top-k value, temperature). A reviewer cannot tell whether the comparison is fair.
- The "frequency-based heuristic" is plausible but obscure as written; one sentence defining it precisely would help.

**Internal tension with Section 5**

The closing paragraph acknowledges that temperature must be re-fit per task family, which directly undercuts the earlier "principled" and "transferable" claims. A reviewer will catch this. Either (i) soften the main claims to "calibrated within a task family, with cross-family generalization an open question," or (ii) add an experiment showing cross-family transfer with and without re-fitting, and report the degradation.

The final sentence — "we expect the calibration gains to transfer cleanly as code LLMs continue to scale" — contradicts the per-task-family caveat and has no supporting experiment. Cut it or replace with a measured statement.

**Specific lines to revise**

- "The novelty of CalibSynth lies in its ability to communicate uncertainty…" → many systems do this; reframe as "in providing well-calibrated per-program confidence at low overhead."
- "clear step forward over standard code-generation pipelines" → reviewers dislike this register; state the delta numerically instead.
- "We take this as evidence that CalibSynth represents the first principled treatment…" → remove or qualify heavily.
- "roughly 200 lines of additional PyTorch" → fine as a footnote, but as a contribution it reads thin; consider moving to the implementation section.
- "We believe this lightweight footprint will encourage adoption" → speculative; cut.

**Structure**

Consider this ordering: (1) problem and why uncalibrated code LLMs are insufficient for deferral; (2) approach in one paragraph; (3) headline empirical result with one quantitative sentence; (4) scope and limitations (per-task-family fitting) up front rather than tucked at the end. Putting the limitation early earns reviewer trust and disarms the contradiction noted above.

**Citations to add**

At minimum: Guo et al. 2017 (temperature scaling); Geifman & El-Yaniv on selective prediction; a representative prior calibration-for-code or verifier-reranking paper (e.g., AlphaCode, CodeT, or Inala et al. on fault-aware neural code rankers); HumanEval (Chen et al. 2021) if that benchmark is used.

Uncertainty disclosure: I have not verified that no prior work specifically labels itself as "calibrated program synthesis"; the novelty critique is based on the broad surrounding literature being well-established, not on a targeted search. Specific cited works (AlphaCode, CodeT, Inala et al.) are named from memory and should be confirmed before inclusion.
